title: Collaborative Filtering with Temporal Dynamics
venue: Proceedings of the 15th ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2009)
research_question: Can collaborative filtering model stable preference, gradual drift, and short-lived temporal effects without discarding useful older ratings?
method: The paper extends matrix factorization with time-varying user and item biases, slowly changing user factors, and day-specific user effects, then evaluates chronologically on the Netflix Prize data.
population_or_data: Netflix Prize movie-rating data with roughly 100 million timestamped ratings; probe and test instances contain more than 1.4 million ratings each and occur after their corresponding training histories.
core_finding: Explicit temporal components improved future-rating RMSE over static SVD and SVD++ variants; the analysis also found that simply downweighting old ratings could remove enduring signal along with stale signal.
architecture_implications: Represent an owner with separate stable, slowly varying, and transient components instead of overwriting one undifferentiated profile.
Evaluate profile updates on future-time holdouts and preserve older evidence unless a model can identify which component actually changed.
limitations: The evidence comes from one movie-rating domain with explicit scalar feedback and a retrospective offline split. Its temporal functions are hand-designed and predictive; they do not establish why a preference changed or whether an owner endorses the inferred change.

"Collaborative Filtering with Temporal Dynamics" full paper Netflix temporal effects

title: Temporal Recommendation on Graphs via Long- and Short-term Preference Fusion
venue: Proceedings of the 16th ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2010)
research_question: Does combining long-term graph preference with session-level short-term preference improve temporal recommendation?
method: The paper constructs session-based temporal graphs and an Injected Preference Fusion procedure that blends long- and short-term graph signals.
population_or_data: A citation network and a social-bookmarking dataset reported by the paper; the accessible abstract does not expose sample sizes.
core_finding: The fused long- and short-term model reportedly improved recommendation quality by 15% to 34% over the compared methods across the two datasets.
architecture_implications: Maintain a durable owner model and a separately expiring session or episode model, with an explicit fusion rule at retrieval time.
Log which temporal layer contributed to a recommendation so a short-lived context cannot silently become a durable preference.
limitations: This record is based on abstract inspection, so implementation details, exact sample sizes, and per-baseline results were not independently inspected. Citation and bookmarking events are not private conversational evidence, and predictive gains do not show that the latent states correspond to owner-recognized preferences.

"Temporal Recommendation on Graphs" KDD 2010 abstract

title: Factorizing Personalized Markov Chains for Next-Basket Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the 19th International Conference on World Wide Web (WWW 2010)
research_question: Can a recommender jointly model a person's long-term taste and the short-term transition from the immediately preceding basket?
method: Factorized Personalized Markov Chains factorize a user-specific item-transition tensor and optimize personalized ranking, combining matrix-factorization preference with first-order Markov transitions.
population_or_data: An anonymized drug-store purchase corpus with 71,602 users, 7,180 items, and 233,476 baskets, plus a denser 10,000-user, 1,002-item subset; each user's final basket is held out.
core_finding: The joint personalized-transition model outperformed matrix factorization, a shared factorized Markov chain, and a conventional Markov chain on both sparse and dense next-basket tests.
architecture_implications: Keep enduring affinity and immediate transition context as separate estimators that can contribute independently to a prediction.
Store transition-derived state with a short validity window rather than promoting the latest action directly into the durable owner profile.
limitations: The model uses only first-order relative basket order and ignores absolute elapsed time and broader context. Results come from one retail corpus, and purchases are behavior traces rather than confirmed preferences or identity claims.

title: Recurrent Recommender Networks
venue: Proceedings of the Tenth ACM International Conference on Web Search and Data Mining (WSDM 2017)
research_question: Can recurrent user and item states capture nonlinear evolution that static or manually parameterized temporal recommenders miss?
method: The model couples LSTM trajectories for users and items with stationary latent factors and evaluates ratings that occur after the training period.
population_or_data: Approximately 1.4 million IMDb ratings and 100 million Netflix ratings, partitioned chronologically into training and future evaluation periods.
core_finding: The recurrent temporal model produced lower future-rating error than the compared probabilistic matrix factorization, TimeSVD++, and AutoRec variants on both datasets.
architecture_implications: Use an event-conditioned state transition for time-varying representations while retaining a stationary component for durable traits.
Version recurrent state checkpoints and retain the triggering evidence so an owner-visible system can reconstruct how a state changed.
limitations: The experiments cover explicit movie ratings and known recurring users and items, not open-ended personal evidence. Learned recurrent states are opaque and predictive; the paper provides no owner correction, causal interpretation, or audit mechanism.

title: Deep Coevolutionary Network: Embedding User and Item Features for Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the 22nd ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2016)
research_question: Can event-driven neural point processes model mutually evolving user and item representations and predict both the next interaction and its timing?
method: DeepCoevolve updates coupled recurrent user and item embeddings at interaction events and uses their compatibility in a temporal point-process intensity.
population_or_data: IPTV viewing data with 7,100 users, 436 shows, and about 2 million events over 11 months; 291,716 Yelp reviews from 1,005 users and 47,924 businesses; and 14,816 Reddit discussions from 1,000 users and 1,403 groups.
core_finding: The event-driven coevolution model improved next-item and event-time prediction over the paper's parametric point-process and recommendation baselines across the three domains.
architecture_implications: Treat interaction events as inputs to a temporal state transition rather than periodically rebuilding an owner vector from an unordered history.
Keep user-state and object-state changes distinct, because an observed shift can arise from changes in either side of an interaction.
limitations: Embeddings change only at observed events and remain opaque between events; compatibility is not an owner-readable preference claim. The three offline platform datasets do not test explicit corrections, consent, or whether inferred evolution matches a person's own account.

title: Predicting Dynamic Embedding Trajectory in Temporal Interaction Networks
venue: Proceedings of the 25th ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2019)
research_question: Can coupled dynamic embeddings project a user's state forward through inactivity and improve future-interaction and state-change prediction?
method: JODIE uses coupled recurrent updates for user and item embeddings, projects user embeddings over elapsed time, and introduces t-Batch for temporally valid parallel training.
population_or_data: Four timestamped interaction networks from Reddit, Wikipedia, a MOOC, and LastFM, evaluated on future interaction and user-state-change tasks.
core_finding: Across six experiments, JODIE improved future-interaction mean reciprocal rank by at least 20% and state-change AUC by 12% on average over the compared baselines, while t-Batch accelerated training.
architecture_implications: Represent elapsed time explicitly when projecting an owner state; inactivity should not be treated as an ordinary observed event.
Attach every dynamic-state update to its event and previous version so projected state can be distinguished from evidence-backed state.
limitations: The latent trajectory is optimized for platform actions and status labels, not for interpretable or owner-confirmed personal change. Projection assumes learned temporal regularity and the evaluation does not test corrections, missing-not-at-random silence, or private conversational contexts.

search_queries: "Predicting Dynamic Embedding Trajectory" DOI KDD 2019
arXiv 1908.01207 JODIE temporal interaction networks

title: Time Interval Aware Self-Attention for Sequential Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the 13th International Conference on Web Search and Data Mining (WSDM 2020)
research_question: Do personalized relative time intervals add useful signal beyond item order and absolute sequence position?
method: TiSASRec adds learned relative time-interval embeddings to self-attention over timestamped item sequences and uses leave-last-out next-item evaluation.
population_or_data: Six public datasets: MovieLens-1M, Amazon CDs and Vinyl, Amazon Movies and TV, Amazon Beauty, Amazon Video Games, and Steam, spanning roughly 0.2 million to 1.05 million interactions each.
core_finding: Time-aware attention generally improved Hit@10 and NDCG@10 over sequential baselines, but the analysis found no obvious sequential pattern for Amazon Beauty, showing that temporal signal strength is domain-dependent.
architecture_implications: Encode relative intervals separately from event order and let the model learn whether recency matters for each context.
Require a temporal-signal check before enabling decay or drift logic; some domains may be better served by a stable model.
limitations: The benchmark treats reviews or ratings as implicit events and evaluates one held-out next item, not enduring owner preferences. Intervals are discretized and capped, and timestamps alone cannot explain whether a gap reflects changed preference, opportunity, or availability.

"TiSASRec" MovieLens Amazon Steam datasets full paper

title: GraphSAIL: Graph Structure Aware Incremental Learning for Recommender Systems
venue: Proceedings of the 29th ACM International Conference on Information and Knowledge Management (CIKM 2020)
research_question: Can incremental graph recommenders learn new interactions while preserving useful old graph structure at lower cost than full retraining?
method: GraphSAIL distills each prior graph model's self, local-neighborhood, and global-cluster structure into the next incrementally trained model.
population_or_data: Timestamp-split Gowalla and LastFM user-item graphs tested with two GNN recommenders, plus 22 consecutive days of industrial mobile app-store interaction logs.
core_finding: GraphSAIL usually achieved the best average Recall@20 while training much faster than batch retraining; on industrial logs it reduced log loss by 5.84% relative to batch training at about 3.5% of the training time.
architecture_implications: Before committing a profile update, compare old and new representations at self, neighborhood, and global levels rather than checking only current-task accuracy.
Preservation losses should be component-wise and observable because an ablation showed that a local-structure term can hurt in at least one model-data pairing.
limitations: Preserving graph structure can also preserve stale or unwanted behavior, and the method has no owner-level retention or deletion policy. The industrial dataset is only briefly described, updates occur in fixed blocks, and most evidence is offline recommendation quality rather than semantic profile fidelity.

title: ADER: Adaptively Distilled Exemplar Replay Towards Continual Learning for Session-based Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the 14th ACM Conference on Recommender Systems (RecSys 2020)
research_question: Can adaptive distillation and a bounded exemplar memory balance new-pattern learning against preservation in incremental recommenders?
method: ADER selects a fixed-budget exemplar set by herding, allocates memory according to recent item frequency, and adapts a distillation weight from old and new data sizes.
population_or_data: DIGINETICA with 993,483 actions split into 17 weekly blocks and YOOCHOOSE with 3,370,578 actions split into daily blocks, evaluated over 16 incremental updates with SASRec.
core_finding: ADER outperformed fine-tuning, dropout, elastic weight consolidation, and joint-training comparisons overall, with larger gains on the more dynamic DIGINETICA stream; on stable YOOCHOOSE, plain fine-tuning already forgot little and EWC added only marginal benefit.
architecture_implications: Allocate a bounded replay budget explicitly and adapt preservation pressure to observed drift instead of applying a fixed anti-forgetting penalty.
Track coverage of rare owner-important evidence, because frequency-proportional exemplars can protect common behavior while dropping infrequent commitments.
limitations: The datasets contain anonymous shopping sessions rather than persistent people, so their item-frequency drift is only an analogue for personal change. A fixed 30,000-example memory and frequency-based allocation introduce retention, privacy, and minority-evidence tradeoffs not evaluated by the paper.

title: Memory Augmented Neural Model for Incremental Session-based Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the Twenty-Ninth International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2020)
research_question: Can a continuously updated nonparametric memory help an incremental session recommender learn new and infrequent items without destructive retraining?
method: MAN combines a neural session recommender with a queried and updated nonparametric memory through a lightweight learned gate.
population_or_data: YOOCHOOSE with about 6.2 million training events over 22,594 items and DIGINETICA with about 636,000 events over 42,294 items, including explicit evaluation on new-item events.
core_finding: The memory-augmented NARM variant was strongest overall and particularly improved new and infrequent items; small incremental updates helped, while large update rates or repeated passes increased forgetting and smaller memories degraded results.
architecture_implications: Route fast-changing evidence through a bounded, inspectable memory path before consolidating it into slower neural parameters.
Expose the gate contribution and memory provenance so a response can distinguish recent-example retrieval from durable model knowledge.
limitations: The strongest configuration benefits from large or unbounded memory, creating storage, privacy, deletion, and audit costs. The data are short anonymous commerce sessions, and one-event-at-a-time offline evaluation does not establish long-term personal continuity.

title: Getting to Know You: Learning New User Preferences in Recommender Systems
venue: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI 2002)
research_question: Which initial-rating questions best balance recommendation accuracy, user effort, and catalog coverage for a new user?
method: The study compares six elicitation strategies using offline simulations on MovieLens and a live MovieLens deployment with more than 300 new users.
population_or_data: About 7.5 million historical MovieLens ratings for simulation and more than 300 newly registering users in the online study.
core_finding: Popularity, popularity-times-entropy, and item-item personalized strategies offered useful accuracy-effort tradeoffs, whereas pure entropy performed poorly in a pilot; the paper also warns that popularity can concentrate exposure and personalization can narrow coverage.
architecture_implications: Choose owner questions by expected information gain, answerability, interaction cost, and coverage rather than uncertainty alone.
Inject limited exploration and audit which domains are never queried so elicitation does not reinforce an already narrow profile.
limitations: The study concerns movie ratings during signup, not corrections or drift in a mature longitudinal profile. Known-item ratings are easier to elicit than nuanced personal beliefs, and the short interaction does not measure durable satisfaction or burden.

title: Active Collaborative Filtering
venue: Proceedings of the Nineteenth Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2003)
research_question: Can expected value of information identify ratings whose answers are most likely to improve a user's recommendation decision?
method: The paper derives myopic expected-value-of-information queries for probabilistic collaborative filters and uses offline query prototypes to reduce online computation.
population_or_data: A filtered EachMovie subset with 5,831 users and 1,003 movies derived from 74,424 users and 1,649 movies.
core_finding: Expected-value-of-information queries reduced model loss more than random and entropy-based querying, especially with sparse observations; prototype sets pruned roughly 60% or 80% of candidate queries, with the 60% condition tracking unpruned performance closely and the 80% condition losing more while still beating the alternative strategies.
architecture_implications: Ask only when an answer is expected to change an owner-facing decision, not merely because a belief is uncertain.
Precompute candidate question families, but record the utility and cost assumptions used to select each owner query.
limitations: The one-step objective can miss combinations of questions whose joint value is high even when each question looks weak alone. The method assumes an explicit probabilistic preference model, rateable items, and query-cost estimates validated only on offline movie data.

title: Personalized Active Learning for Collaborative Filtering
venue: Proceedings of the 31st Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval (SIGIR 2008)
research_question: Can active-learning queries be personalized to both model informativeness and the chance that a particular user can answer?
method: A Bayesian aspect model ranks candidate items by informativeness multiplied by estimated user-specific rateability and compares ten active-query rounds with Bayesian and random alternatives.
population_or_data: A 500-user, 1,000-movie MovieRating subset and MovieLens with 943 users and 1,682 movies; test users begin with three observed ratings.
core_finding: Personalized query selection improved recommendation accuracy and reduced unanswerable queries to roughly five or six per ten, versus more than nine for the non-personalized comparisons.
architecture_implications: Estimate whether an owner can reasonably answer a question and combine that probability with expected information value.
Treat skips and 'not sure' as query-design feedback rather than negative evidence about the underlying preference.
limitations: Offline missing ratings are used as a proxy for inability to answer, which can conflate exposure with knowledge. The old movie datasets, small latent model, and ten-query protocol do not measure live burden or preference drift over months or years.

title: Learning Preferences of New Users in Recommender Systems: An Information Theoretic Approach
venue: ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter, Volume 10, Issue 2
research_question: How do information-theoretic item-selection rules trade recommendation quality against actual new-user effort?
method: The paper combines offline simulations with a 20-day live MovieLens experiment comparing system-selected information-gain variants, popularity, and user-selected ratings.
population_or_data: About 11,000 MovieLens users, 9,000 movies, and 3 million ratings; 468 people registered for the live experiment, 381 completed it, and 305 answered the exit survey.
core_finding: A system-controlled information-gain strategy gave the best overall accuracy while popularity performed worst; all conditions required manageable effort, and measured effort differences were not always reflected in users' subjective reports.
architecture_implications: Evaluate elicitation with both downstream model improvement and observed interaction burden; self-reported effort alone can miss friction.
Offer owner-selected and system-selected correction paths, because an efficient algorithmic query policy does not replace autonomy over what to disclose.
limitations: The study covers 20 days of movie onboarding and does not observe established-profile drift or repeated correction cycles. Its candidate questions depend on an existing community rating matrix and may not transfer to sparse, private, or unique personal facts.

title: Open User Profiles for Adaptive News Systems: Help or Harm?
venue: Proceedings of the 16th International Conference on World Wide Web (WWW 2007)
research_question: Does exposing and editing an adaptive news profile improve recommendation quality and user experience?
method: A within-participant study compares a hidden profile with an editable keyword profile in YourNews across two timed news-finding sessions.
population_or_data: Ten graduate students, two 15-minute tasks per participant, and a 456-article news corpus used to establish relevance judgments.
core_finding: Participants preferred profile visibility and control, but editing reduced objective system and user precision and recall; more edits, especially removals, were associated with worse performance.
architecture_implications: Treat an owner edit as a proposed model delta with a preview of affected behavior, validation against held-out evidence, and one-step rollback.
Separate 'hide this now' from 'delete this durable belief' so a novelty complaint cannot accidentally erase an enduring interest.
limitations: The study has only ten participants, short tasks, and a keyword news model, so effect estimates are fragile. Novelty and redundancy problems may have motivated edits, confounding preference correction with interface-level frustration.

title: TasteWeights: A Visual Interactive Hybrid Recommender System
venue: Proceedings of the Sixth ACM Conference on Recommender Systems (RecSys 2012)
research_question: Can visible recommendation provenance and direct weighting controls improve perceived quality and relevance?
method: TasteWeights visualizes social and semantic recommendation sources, lets users adjust source and item weights with immediate feedback, and compares interactive and noninteractive conditions.
population_or_data: Thirty-two participants using their own Facebook music preferences and social connections in a supervised laboratory study.
core_finding: Interactive control increased reported satisfaction and recommendation relevance, but the paper notes that the accuracy comparison is not fully fair because feedback collected during interaction also informed the resulting recommendations.
architecture_implications: Expose source-level provenance and let owners adjust influence weights with an immediate preview rather than editing opaque latent parameters.
Log whether a quality gain came from a corrected model or from extra feedback gathered during the control interaction.
limitations: The sample is small, the session is short, and the Facebook-dependent music setting is platform-specific and now operationally dated. Control and additional feedback are confounded, and importing social connections raises third-party privacy concerns not resolved by the interface.

title: An Approach to Controlling User Models and Personalization Effects in Recommender Systems
venue: Proceedings of the 18th International Conference on Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI 2013)
research_question: How do profile visualization, editing, explanations, overrides, and a personalization switch affect perceived control in a professional recommender?
method: A two-phase field study first exposes a personalized literature portal without controls and then adds user-model inspection, editing, explanations, recommendation overrides, and on-off controls.
population_or_data: Seven employees of a genomics research center used a biochemical literature portal for two consecutive two-week phases.
core_finding: Participants reported improved usefulness, transparency, and controllability after controls were introduced, while trust and privacy concerns remained.
architecture_implications: Provide separate controls to inspect, edit, override, and disable personalization; one generic correction button does not cover these different intents.
Persist explanations and owner actions in an audit log so a user can see which model version and rule produced an effect.
limitations: Only seven participants were studied, with no control group or counterbalancing, so phase order and novelty can explain part of the improvement. The four-week professional-document setting does not demonstrate durable behavior, objective accuracy gains, or safe correction at scale.

title: Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting in Neural Networks
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Volume 114, Issue 13
research_question: Can a neural network learn sequential tasks while selectively protecting parameters important to earlier tasks?
method: Elastic Weight Consolidation adds a quadratic penalty weighted by a diagonal Fisher-information estimate of each parameter's importance to prior tasks.
population_or_data: Permuted-MNIST task sequences and a sequence of ten Atari 2600 reinforcement-learning games.
core_finding: EWC retained substantially more prior-task performance than ordinary sequential training and dropout while learning new tasks, although the single continual Atari agent remained below separate game-specific agents.
architecture_implications: Estimate which parameters or profile components support validated old behavior and apply component-specific stability constraints during updates.
Keep an explicit pre-update evaluation suite because a regularizer reduces forgetting risk but does not guarantee retention.
limitations: The method assumes task changes or context that can be represented during consolidation and approximates parameter uncertainty with a diagonal Fisher matrix. Permuted digits and Atari games are far from ambiguous personal preference drift, and the penalty provides no semantic audit trail or owner deletion mechanism.

search_queries: "Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks" PNAS PMC
doi 10.1073/pnas.1611835114 elastic weight consolidation

title: Gradient Episodic Memory for Continual Learning
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 30 (NeurIPS 2017)
research_question: Can small episodic memories constrain updates so new-task learning does not increase loss on remembered examples from earlier tasks?
method: GEM stores examples per task and projects each gradient into a feasible region where loss on every prior-task memory does not increase; it also defines average accuracy, backward transfer, and forward transfer metrics.
population_or_data: Twenty-task permuted- and rotated-MNIST streams and incremental CIFAR-100, with each training example observed once.
core_finding: GEM was competitive across the continual image benchmarks, limited forgetting, and sometimes produced positive backward transfer while using a bounded episodic memory.
architecture_implications: Gate every proposed model update against regression checks on a curated set of earlier owner-approved anchors before commit.
Report backward and forward transfer by temporal slice so an update policy exposes both preservation and beneficial revision.
limitations: The method requires task descriptors, stores examples selected by a simple recency rule, and performs a backward pass for each prior task on every update. Image-classification task boundaries and labels do not match gradual, overlapping, or contradictory changes in a personal model.

search_queries: "Gradient Episodic Memory for Continual Learning" NeurIPS 2017
arXiv 1706.08840 GEM continual learning full paper

title: Episodic Memory in Lifelong Language Learning
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (NeurIPS 2019)
research_question: Can sparse replay and temporary local adaptation reduce forgetting in a stream of language tasks without dataset identifiers?
method: The model stores key-value episodic examples, sparsely replays them during training, retrieves similar examples at inference, and performs local adaptation whose parameter changes are then discarded.
population_or_data: Streams assembled from text-classification and question-answering datasets without exposing dataset identity to the learner.
core_finding: Sparse replay and retrieval-time local adaptation provided complementary gains over the compared lifelong-learning baselines; random memory subsampling reduced storage by roughly 50% to 90% with little reported performance loss.
architecture_implications: Use retrieved episodic evidence for temporary, request-scoped adaptation before deciding whether any change deserves durable consolidation.
Apply explicit retention and deletion rules to replay memory because stored text examples can carry more sensitive detail than model parameters alone.
limitations: The streams concatenate labeled benchmark datasets with known output spaces rather than natural, owner-driven preference changes. Example memory creates privacy and deletion obligations, local adaptation adds inference cost, and large retrieval sets caused memory limits on question answering.

search_queries: "Episodic Memory in Lifelong Language Learning" NeurIPS 2019
arXiv 1906.01076 sparse replay local adaptation
